title: Image Analysis with AWS Rekognition: Blunted Heels
excerpt: Using AWS Rekognition and IAM access keys to extract image labels and bounding boxes from an artisanal avant-garde shoe design — and what a 73% confidence score for "Art" says about machine perception.
body:
Using AWS's AI image service, Rekognition, and IAM access keys I was able to get technically accurate information back from terminal command line along with image labels.

Technologies Used

  • AWS Rekognition
  • AWS IAM
  • S3 Bucket
  • Python

S3 and Access Keys

Uploading the image(s) into an Amazon S3 bucket, which will be the source for analyzing and generating labels by Amazon Rekognition. The AWS Command Line Interface will be used for interacting with the AWS Services. IAM roles and granted access keys are used to provide user authentication and access to the Amazon CLI on a local system through terminal.

#!/usr/bin/env python3.11

import boto3
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as patches
from PIL import Image
from io import BytesIO

def detect_labels(photo, bucket):
    client = boto3.client('rekognition')

    response = client.detect_labels(
        Image={'S3Object': {'Bucket':'', 'Name':''}},  ## Name of Bucket and Image file
        MaxLabels=10)

    print('Detected labels for ' + photo)
    print()

    for label in response['Labels']:
        print("Label:", label['Name'])
        print("Confidence:", label['Confidence'])
        print()

    # Load the image from S3
    s3 = boto3.resource('s3')
    obj = s3.Object(bucket, photo)
    img_data = obj.get()['Body'].read()
    img = Image.open(BytesIO(img_data))

    plt.imshow(img)
    ax = plt.gca()

    # Plot bounding boxes
    for label in response['Labels']:
        for instance in label.get('Instances', []):
            bbox = instance['BoundingBox']
            left   = bbox['Left']   * img.width
            top    = bbox['Top']    * img.height
            width  = bbox['Width']  * img.width
            height = bbox['Height'] * img.height

            rect = patches.Rectangle(
                (left, top), width, height,
                linewidth=1, edgecolor='r', facecolor='none'
            )
            ax.add_patch(rect)

            label_text = label['Name'] + ' (' + str(round(label['Confidence'], 2)) + '%)'
            plt.text(left, top - 2, label_text, color='r', fontsize=8,
                     bbox=dict(facecolor='white', alpha=0.7))

    plt.axis('off')
    plt.show()
    return len(response['Labels'])

def main():
    photo  = ''
    bucket = ''
    label_count = detect_labels(photo, bucket)
    print("Labels detected:", label_count)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

The Python script consists of the programming logic for extracting the image from S3 and running the detect labels operation from Rekognition. It generates a pop-up window displaying the image with bounding boxes drawn around each detected label.

Reflections & Analysis

I was taken aback by the findings from the experimentation, which claimed the artisanal avant-garde design had a confidence level of 73.89% of being classified as art. With my background in art, I have been exposed to arguments like what constitutes art? and what are the limitations of design in the context of art? before. Because I self-manufactured and designed the shoes from scratch, I would agree that to a certain extent they are "Art" — if not by the simple basis that the wearer would have to be wholly subject to the shoes and not the other way around (they are built to be unstable).

This was interesting because the AI also picked up on the fact that they are sculptural and slightly removed from the world of functionality in design, which is integral to the design landscape.

Takeaways

I plan to continue this line of research, testing different images that blur the lines of concise definition — where the classification judgements called for in the script would be useful to help drive a conversation. I would then think about formalizing that data in an effort to gather insights about the AI image detection range and discover potential limitations with respect to picking up subtleties, in a future experiment.

title: Serverless AI-Generated TTS: AWS Lambda + Polly
excerpt: Using serverless cloud architecture (AWS Lambda) coupled with AWS Polly, I generated an audio file reciting Baudelaire's Parfum Exotique — in seconds, with zero servers to manage.
body:
Using serverless cloud architecture (AWS Lambda) coupled with AWS's AI Text-To-Speech service, Polly, I was able to generate an audio file reciting a text piece in a matter of seconds.

Technologies Used

  • AWS Lambda
  • AWS IAM
  • AWS S3
  • AWS Polly
  • Python 3.11

Text File, IAM Permissions, and S3 Bucket Setup

To begin, I gathered my plaintext file — Charles Baudelaire's Parfum Exotique — to get started with my S3 Bucket (created for my region), which would act as the source for the Lambda function to deploy. I then went to IAM to create a role with the use case of "Lambda" and added permissions: AmazonPollyReadOnlyAccess and AmazonS3FullAccess.

Lambda Function

The Lambda function uses the Python 3.11 runtime. Under the "Change default execution role" tab I applied the IAM role I created. Below is the deployed script:

import json
import boto3
import datetime

current_time = datetime.datetime.now()
timestamp = current_time.strftime("%d%m%y")

s3_client    = boto3.client('s3')
polly_client = boto3.client('polly')

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    source_bucket    = str(event["Records"][0]['s3']['bucket']['name'])
    source_file_key  = str(event["Records"][0]['s3']['object']['key'])
    destination_bucket   = 'destination_bucket'  ## Update with your bucket name
    destination_file_key = f"{timestamp}-audio.mp3"

    # Fetch text from S3
    try:
        response = s3_client.get_object(Bucket=source_bucket, Key=source_file_key)
        text = response['Body'].read().decode('utf-8')
    except Exception as e:
        return {'statusCode': 500, 'body': f'Error fetching text: {str(e)}'}

    # Synthesize speech with Polly
    try:
        response   = polly_client.synthesize_speech(
            OutputFormat='mp3',
            Text=text,
            VoiceId='Salli'
        )
        audio_data = response['AudioStream'].read()
    except Exception as e:
        return {'statusCode': 500, 'body': f'Error with Polly: {str(e)}'}

    # Upload MP3 to destination bucket
    try:
        s3_client.put_object(
            Bucket=destination_bucket,
            Key=destination_file_key,
            Body=audio_data
        )
    except Exception as e:
        return {'statusCode': 500, 'body': f'Error uploading MP3: {str(e)}'}

    print("success")
    return {
        'statusCode': 200,
        'body': f'Saved to s3://{destination_bucket}/{destination_file_key}'
    }

The script processes the contents of the source bucket, passes the text through Polly to create a client object, and exports the result to a destination bucket. Note: update destination_bucket with your second bucket's name.

Triggering the Lambda

To link this Lambda function to the source bucket, I clicked "Create Notifications" on the first S3 bucket, setting the destination to the ttspeech Lambda function. This triggers processing automatically whenever a .txt file is uploaded. Saving the changes is the last step of this automated pipeline.

Result

Upon uploading the file to the source S3 bucket, I was greeted with Baudelaire's Parfum Exotique recited by Salli, waiting in the destination bucket as an .mp3 file. Listen to the output here.

Potential Applications

  • TTS phone notifications while driving or when hands are occupied
  • Converting blog posts and long-form text into audio files
  • Accessibility tooling for content-heavy sites

Through Lambda's serverless computing, I set up an automated pipeline deployed with Python using S3 as a client — filtering a text file into a newly generated .mp3 file in a destination bucket, ready to download and share.
